quiz.question: If your investments dropped 20% in a month, what would you do?

answers.balanced: Wait and see — stick to the plan
answers.adventurous: Buy more while prices are low

Landing.subtitle: A friendly guide that builds — and explains — an investing plan you actually understand.
Landing.mascotAlt: Sprout, the Future You mascot, waving with open arms
Landing.apiStatusError: Couldn't reach the engine just yet — it may be waking up.
Landing.placeholderNote: Phase 0 — setup check. The real experience is built next.

more.label: More
more.title: More
more.description: Jump to another part of Future You.

rail.title: Your status
rail.planHealthCaption: Answer a few questions to see how healthy your plan is.
rail.investedCaption: You haven't set up a plan yet.
rail.nextReviewCaption: We'll suggest a date once your plan exists.
rail.highlightBody: A few quick questions, and you'll have a plan you actually understand.

utility.title: New to investing?
utility.body: Start with the basics and learn as you go.

demo.title: Welcome to Future You
demo.lead: This is a preview of the app shell — the sidebar, status rail, and bottom tab bar that wrap every screen. The real pages arrive next.
demo.cardPlanBody: Once you answer a few questions, your personalized mix lands here — with the why behind every choice.
demo.cardLearnBody: Short, plain-language lessons turn each part of your plan into something you understand.
demo.howProfileBody: A few quick questions about your situation.
demo.howGoalBody: What you're saving toward, and by when.
demo.howRiskBody: How much ups and downs you're okay with.

StyleGuide.title: Design tokens
StyleGuide.intro: The building blocks of Future You — colour, type, shape, and motion. This page is a dev-only reference.
StyleGuide.colorNote: Bold colour on the actionable things; calm cream and white everywhere else. Money never relies on colour alone.
StyleGuide.twoGreenNote: One green can't be both a button fill and readable text at WCAG-AA. So green-600 fills buttons with white text (5.22:1), and green-700 carries any green text or icon on a light background (6.39:1).
StyleGuide.typeNote: Baloo 2 for chunky, friendly headings; Nunito for legible body text and numerals.
StyleGuide.specimen: Invest a little, learn a lot, and watch Future You grow.
StyleGuide.buttonNote: Press it — the hard 4px shadow sinks flat. Our one tactile signature.
StyleGuide.chipNote: Tap to choose — used in the risk quiz and interests.
StyleGuide.numbersNote: Money uses tabular lining figures so columns line up, formatted for your language.
StyleGuide.focusNote: Tab to any control for a visible green focus ring. Every transition respects reduced-motion.
StyleGuide.sproutNote: Your guide on (nearly) every screen — used big, with one distinct pose per moment. Sprout breathes with a soft idle bob and holds still if you prefer reduced motion.
StyleGuide.galleryNote: All 109 pieces, each with bilingual alt text — so nothing ever renders without a description for screen readers.

components.title: Components
components.intro: The reusable building blocks, re-skinned onto the tokens above. Each is shown in the states you'll meet in the product.
components.buttonsNote: Our one tactile signature: a hard 4px edge that sinks flat on press. Every label passes AA — white on the dark fills, ink on the light ones.
components.buttonA11y: White text sits only on the dark fills (green, ink); the light fills (coral, gold, sky, grape) carry ink labels.
components.cardsNote: White by default; soft accent washes for variety; one dark ink→grape highlight card per page for a single call-out.
components.cardDefaultBody: A plain white card — the everyday unit of content.
components.cardSkyBody: A soft sky wash for a gentle, informational aside.
components.cardGrapeBody: A grape wash for variety and tertiary notes.
components.cardCoralBody: A coral wash to warmly draw the eye.
components.cardHighlightBody: The one dark highlight a page may use — white text clears AA across the whole gradient.
components.chipsNote: Tap to choose. Selected chips fill with the green tint, gain a green border and a check, and give a small bounce (silent under reduced motion).
components.chipsRiskLabel: How do you feel about risk?
components.chipsInterestsLabel: What are you curious about? (choose any)
components.controlsNote: Friendly 48px fields, a CHF money field with tabular figures, and a segmented control for the short / medium / long horizon.
components.badgesNote: Small labels, and the red count counters from the Learn and Research menus.
components.overlaysNote: Dialogs, sheets, tooltips, tabs and accordions — over a warm ink scrim, focus-trapped, keyboard-friendly, and quiet under reduced motion.
components.ovDialogBody: We'll ask a few quick questions, then show a plan you can actually understand — and change anytime.
components.ovSheetDesc: Jump to another part of Future You.
components.ovTabOverviewBody: A pill tab track: the active tab is a raised white pill, never colour alone.
components.ovTabHoldingsBody: Eight funds, diversified across regions and sectors.
components.ovTabAboutBody: A plain-language summary of what this plan does and why.
components.ovAccA1: Your returns earn returns. Each year's growth joins next year's base, so a small amount can snowball over a long horizon.
components.ovAccA2: Spreading money across many holdings so one bad surprise can't sink the whole plan — many baskets, not one.
components.ovAccQ3: Why hold bonds as well as stocks?
components.ovAccA3: Bonds usually swing less than stocks, so they steady the ride when markets dip.

TeachingPrimitives.title: How Future You explains things
TeachingPrimitives.intro: Two building blocks for learning as you invest — the answer in plain words, with the maths one tap away, and any jargon defined on the spot.
TeachingPrimitives.equationNote: Every number leads with a plain-language answer. Open "Show the maths" only if you want to see exactly how it's worked out.
TeachingPrimitives.eqResult: Start with CHF 1,000, add CHF 100 a month, and if it grows about 5% a year, in 20 years you'd have about CHF 43,800 — even though you only put in CHF 25,000 yourself.
TeachingPrimitives.eqFormulaAlt: Future value equals P times one plus r, all raised to the power n, plus C times the quantity one plus r to the power n minus one, divided by r.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymFV: Future value — what your savings could grow to.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymP: Principal — what you start with today (CHF 1,000).
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 100).
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymR: Monthly growth rate — your ~5% a year, split across 12 months.
TeachingPrimitives.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested (20 years = 240).
TeachingPrimitives.eqExampleAlt: Using the example numbers, the future value works out to about 43,800 Swiss francs.
TeachingPrimitives.eqExampleCaption: You put in CHF 25,000 of your own — the other ~CHF 18,800 is growth doing the work.
TeachingPrimitives.eqWhy: Each year's growth earns its own growth the next year — that snowball is compound interest, and time is what makes it big.
TeachingPrimitives.glossaryIntro: Any underlined word has a plain-language definition — tap it to see it, without leaving the page.
TeachingPrimitives.glossaryParagraph: An <etf>ETF</etf> lets you own a whole basket of investments in one go. Spreading your money this way — <diversification>diversification</diversification> — softens the blow when any single holding drops. It won't remove <volatility>volatility</volatility>, the normal up-and-down swing of prices, but it makes the ride smoother.
TeachingPrimitives.defEtf: A single fund you can buy that holds a whole basket of investments at once — instant variety, at low cost.
TeachingPrimitives.defDiversification: Spreading your money across many investments so one bad apple can't sink everything.
TeachingPrimitives.defVolatility: How much an investment's price swings up and down — bigger swings mean a bumpier ride.

Charts.title: Charts that tell the truth
Charts.intro: Every chart answers a plain question, shows a range instead of a false promise, and can open its maths or turn into a table. The figures here are a sample — your real numbers arrive with your plan.
Charts.growthSectionIntro: Where your savings could grow, the odds of reaching your goal, what builds the balance, and how bumpy the ride might be. Sample numbers; your real growth arrives with your plan.
Charts.riskSectionIntro: How bumpy a year could be, whether your holdings move together, where the risk really comes from, and whether you're being paid for it. Sample numbers; your real risk arrives with your plan.
Charts.stockSectionIntro: A closer look at a single holding: its price over time, where analysts think it's headed, and the plain facts about the company. Sample numbers; your real research arrives with your plan.
Charts.scenarioSectionIntro: How a rough year could feel, how holding longer steadies the ride, and whether your mix is ahead of just buying a plain index. These are forward-looking, illustrative scenarios — not a replay of any real crisis. Sample numbers; your real scenarios arrive with your plan.
Charts.viewGroup: Switch between the chart and a table of the same numbers

projection.title: Where could my savings grow?
projection.caption: The green line is the most likely path; the shaded band is the range from a rough run to a good one. The further ahead you look, the wider that range — and the more reaching your goal depends on time.
projection.ariaLabel: A projection of your savings over time: a most-likely line inside a shaded range of good and rough outcomes, with your goal and your starting point marked. The same data is available as a table.
projection.legendRange: Range of outcomes (80% of the time)
projection.tableCaption: The same projection as a table: for each year, a rough run, the most likely amount, and a good run.
projection.eqResult: Starting with CHF 10,000 and adding CHF 300 a month, if it grows about 5% a year, in 15 years the most likely path lands near CHF 100,000 — but the real result lands somewhere across the shaded band.
projection.eqFormulaAlt: Future value equals P times one plus r, all raised to the power n, plus C times the quantity one plus r to the power n minus one, divided by r.
projection.eqSymFV: Future value — the most likely amount your savings could grow to.
projection.eqSymP: Principal — what you start with today (CHF 10,000).
projection.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 300).
projection.eqSymR: Monthly growth rate — about 5% a year, split across 12 months.
projection.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested (15 years = 180).
projection.eqExampleAlt: Using the example numbers, the future value works out to about 100,000 Swiss francs.
projection.eqExampleCaption: This is only the middle line. The band around it comes from how much returns can vary year to year — which is why we never show a single promise.
projection.eqWhyLabel: Why a band, not a line
projection.eqWhy: Nobody knows the exact return each year, so a single line would be a false promise. The band shows the range most outcomes fall in — honest about what we can and can't know.

drawdown.title: How far could it fall along the way?
drawdown.caption: Even a strong plan dips. This is the “stomach test”: how far the path fell from its most recent high — the deepest fall you'd have had to sit through — before climbing back. The growth chart shows how high it could go; this shows the bumps to hold through on the way.
drawdown.ariaLabel: An underwater plot of how far the projected path falls from its most recent high over time: a soft area dipping below a 0% line, with the deepest fall marked. The same data is available as a table.
drawdown.legendFall: How far below its recent peak
drawdown.tableCaption: The deepest dip in each year: how far the path was below its most recent high, its value then, and that recent peak.
drawdown.eqResult: In this sample the deepest fall was about {mdd} — the path slid from a peak of {peak} to {trough} before climbing back. That worst drop from a recent high is the maximum drawdown.
drawdown.eqFormulaAlt: Maximum drawdown equals the smallest value over time of V sub t divided by V peak — the highest value reached so far — minus one.
drawdown.eqSymV: The invested value at a moment in time.
drawdown.eqSymPeak: The highest value reached up to that moment — the running peak.
drawdown.eqSymMdd: Maximum drawdown — the deepest fall from a peak, as a share of that peak.
drawdown.eqExampleAlt: The trough value divided by the peak value, minus one, is about the maximum drawdown percentage.
drawdown.eqWhy: It's the stomach test — not the final return, but the hardest stretch you'd have to hold through without selling. Bigger, longer-held plans usually see deeper dips and still recover.

stats.recoveredYesNote: back to a new high by {year}

goal.title: Will I reach my goal?
goal.caption: The green arc is the share of possible futures that reach your goal by then — the odds, not a promise. Because returns swing year to year, we look at the whole range: the more time you give it, the more of that range clears the line.
goal.ariaLabel: A gauge showing the chance of reaching your goal: a green arc fills the share of possible futures that reach it, with the percentage and a plain-language verdict. The chance for each time frame is available as a table.
goal.tableCaption: The chance of reaching your goal for each time frame, with the most likely amount by then.
goal.eqResult: By {year}, the most likely amount is about {median}. Because real returns vary, we look at the whole range of futures — and about {prob} of them clear your {goal} goal.
goal.eqFormulaAlt: The chance of reaching the goal equals phi of the quantity most-likely amount minus goal, divided by the spread.
goal.eqSymPhi: The share of a bell curve of outcomes that lands at or above your goal.
goal.eqSymM: The most likely amount — the middle of the range of futures.
goal.eqSymG: Your goal — the amount you're aiming for.
goal.eqSymSigma: The spread — how widely the outcomes fan out by then.
goal.eqExampleAlt: Phi of the quantity 101,324 minus 100,000, divided by 24,497, is about 0.52 — that is, 52 percent.
goal.eqExampleCaption: A near coin-flip at 15 years: the most likely amount only just reaches the goal, so about half the range lands above it and half below.
goal.eqWhyLabel: Why odds, not a yes or no
goal.eqWhy: Nobody can promise a single outcome, so we show the odds across the whole range of futures — honest about the chance, never a false guarantee.

stats.gapLabel: The middle path vs your goal

contributions.title: What builds my balance?
contributions.caption: Your balance splits into two: the money you put in, and the money it earned on top. Early on it's mostly your deposits — but give it long enough and compounding does the heavier lifting.
contributions.ariaLabel: A stacked area splitting your balance into the money you put in and the money it earned, year by year, with the point where growth overtakes your deposits marked. The same data is available as a table.
contributions.tableCaption: The same split as a table: for each year, the money you added, the money it earned, and the total balance.
contributions.eqResult: Over {years} years you'd put in about {added}, and compounding would add another {earned} — so more than half of your {total} balance is money your money earned.
contributions.eqFormulaAlt: The total balance equals the money you added, which is the starting amount plus the monthly contribution times the number of months, plus the growth it earned.
contributions.eqSymV: Total balance — what you added plus what it earned.
contributions.eqSymP: Principal — what you start with today (CHF 10,000).
contributions.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 300).
contributions.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested (30 years = 360).
contributions.eqSymG: Growth — the money your money earned on top of your deposits.
contributions.eqExampleAlt: The starting amount plus 300 times 360, plus 176,355 of growth, is about 294,000 Swiss francs.
contributions.eqExampleCaption: By year 30 the green earned band is larger than the blue added band — the growth is now doing more than the deposits.
contributions.eqWhy: Each year's growth earns its own growth the next year. Given enough time, that compounding outgrows the steady deposits — which is why starting early matters more than adding more.

mix.sectionIntro: What you own, how it nests together, which parts carry the weight — and how your version compares to the textbook one. Sample numbers; your real mix arrives with your plan.

donut.title: What am I invested in?
donut.caption: Your money splits across four sleeves: equity grows it, bonds and cash steady the ride, and a diversifier marches to its own beat. The slices are the real weights — they add up to 100%.
donut.ariaLabel: A donut of your mix by sleeve: equity, bonds, cash and a diversifier, each sized by its share. The same data is available as a table.
donut.tableCaption: Your mix by sleeve: each sleeve's share and what it does for you.

role.equity: Grows your money over the long run
role.bond: Steadies the ride when markets dip

stats.splitNote: four core funds, three satellite tilts

sunburst.title: Where does each franc sit?
sunburst.caption: The same money, zoomed in: each sleeve opens into its asset classes, and each asset class into the actual holdings. Rings step lighter as you go outward.
sunburst.ariaLabel: A sunburst of your mix in three rings — sleeve, then asset class, then holding — each arc sized by its share. The same data is available as a table.
sunburst.tableCaption: Every holding, with the sleeve and asset class it sits in and its share of your mix.

treemap.title: Which holdings weigh the most?
treemap.caption: Every holding as a tile. Size it by money and the big funds dominate; switch to risk and a small, jumpy holding can suddenly loom large — weight and risk are not the same thing.
treemap.ariaLabelWeight: A treemap of your holdings, each tile sized by its share of your money. The same data is available as a table.
treemap.ariaLabelRisk: A treemap of your holdings, each tile sized by its share of total portfolio risk. The same data is available as a table.
treemap.tableCaption: Every holding by both measures: its share of your money, and its share of the plan's total risk.

stats.label: Most risk for its size
stats.note: {weight} of the money, {risk} of the risk

optimal.title: What did tilting toward my picks cost me?
optimal.caption: The optimal mix holds only the low-cost core funds. Yours adds three tilts you funded by trimming the core. Here's the trade: a touch more expected return, a bit more bounce.
optimal.ariaLabel: A grouped bar chart comparing, for each holding, the optimal mix weight and your tilted weight. The same data is available as a table.
optimal.tableCaption: Each holding's weight in the optimal mix and in yours, and the difference.
optimal.eqResult: Your mix aims for about {yourR} a year versus {optR} for the optimal mix — but it swings a little more ({yourRisk} versus {optRisk}). So each unit of risk buys slightly less return. That small gap is the price of tilting toward names you believe in.
optimal.eqFormulaAlt: Return per unit of risk, S, equals the expected return R divided by the risk sigma.
optimal.eqSymS: Return per unit of risk — higher means more reward for the same bumps.
optimal.eqSymR: Expected return — the average yearly growth you're aiming for.
optimal.eqSymSigma: Risk — how much the value swings in a typical year (volatility).
optimal.eqExampleAlt: Your return of about 5.5% divided by your risk of about 10.4% is roughly 0.53, versus about 0.54 for the optimal mix.
optimal.eqExampleCaption: Higher is better. Your tilt costs a sliver of this ratio — a small, deliberate trade.
optimal.eqWhy: There's no free lunch: reaching for the extra return your picks might bring also adds a little risk. Worth it if you understand and want that tilt — not if you don't.

distribution.title: How bad could a year be?
distribution.caption: Most years land near the middle, but some are worse. The red bars are the unluckiest 5%. Hold for longer and the good and bad years average out — change the timeframe and watch the tail pull in.
distribution.ariaLabel: A histogram of your likely yearly return, with the worst 5% shaded and two rules marking the bad-year and average-bad-year figures. The same data is available as a table.
distribution.tableCaption: Your likely yearly return, split into ranges, with the chance of landing in each.
distribution.eqResult: Over {years} years, a typical year returns about {mean}. But 1 year in 20 could come in at {var} or worse — and if it does, the average of those bad years is about {cvar}. Hold for longer and the good and bad years average out, so that tail pulls back toward the middle.
distribution.eqFormulaAlt: The bad-year figure, VaR at 95 percent, equals the average return mu plus z (about minus 1.65) times the risk sigma.
distribution.eqSymMu: The average yearly return you're aiming for.
distribution.eqSymSigma: How much a year's return swings around that average (risk).
distribution.eqSymZ: How far into the tail 'the worst 5%' sits — about 1.65 swings below average.
distribution.eqExampleAlt: 5.5% plus minus-1.65 times about 4.7% is roughly minus 2.2%.
distribution.eqExampleCaption: So in a rough year on a 5-year horizon, a typical year might dip about 2%. Over 30 years, that same tail barely goes negative.
distribution.eqWhy: A single year can swing a lot. But averaged over many years, the extremes cancel out — which is why a long horizon makes the same portfolio feel far less risky.

correlation.title: Do my holdings move together?
correlation.caption: Green pairs rise and fall together; grape pairs lean opposite; pale pairs barely notice each other. A mix of pale and grape is what smooths the ride — when one zigs, another zags.
correlation.ariaLabel: A grid showing how strongly each pair of your holdings moves together, from move-apart to move-together. Each cell shows the number. The same data is available as a table.
correlation.tableCaption: Every distinct pair of holdings and how closely they move (−1 opposite, 0 unrelated, +1 in lockstep).

contribution.title: Where does my risk come from?
contribution.caption: The green bar is each holding's share of the plan's risk; the sky bar is its share of your money. When green towers over sky, a small position is doing outsized damage if things go wrong.
contribution.ariaLabel: A grouped bar chart comparing, for each holding, its share of your money and its share of total risk. The same data is available as a table.
contribution.tableCaption: Each holding's share of your money next to its share of the plan's total risk.

stats.driverNote: {risk} of the risk from {weight} of the money

frontier.title: Am I being paid for my risk?
frontier.caption: The green line is the best return you can expect at each level of risk. The optimal mix sits right at the sweet spot; you're a step past it — taking a little more risk that isn't paying quite as well. Single holdings sit below the line: riskier on their own.
frontier.ariaLabel: A scatter plot of risk against expected return, showing the best-return line, the optimal mix, a you-are-here marker, and single holdings. The same data is available as a table.
frontier.tableCaption: Each mix and holding by its risk, its expected return, and its return per unit of risk.

stats.spotNote: a step past the best-paid point

price.title: How has {name} moved?
price.caption: The green line is {name}'s closing price over time. Switch on candlesticks to see each period's open, high, low and close — one candle per period, green when it rose, red when it fell. Change the time frame to zoom from the last month out to its whole history.
price.ariaLabel: A price history of {name}: the closing price over the chosen time frame, with an option to switch to candlesticks. The same data is available as a table.
price.tableCaption: The price over the chosen time frame: each period's open, high, low and close.

analyst.title: Where do analysts think {name} is headed?
analyst.caption: Analysts publish a price target — where they think the stock is worth in a year. The rail spans their lowest to highest call; the gold dot is the average (the consensus), and the ink marker is where it trades today. It's a spread of opinions, not a promise.
analyst.ariaLabel: Analyst price targets for {name}: a low-to-high range with the average consensus and today's price marked, and the implied move to the consensus. The same data is available as a table.
analyst.tableCaption: The analyst targets and today's price, each with the move it implies from today.

fundamentals.title: What kind of company is {name}?
fundamentals.caption: A quick ID card from the facts we hold: how big it is, how much it swings versus the market, how long it's been listed, and a few quality flags. These are the real snapshot figures — nothing here is an estimate.
fundamentals.ariaLabel: A fundamentals snapshot of {name}: how much it moves versus the market, and its quality flags. The same data is available as a table.
fundamentals.betaTitle: How much it moves vs the market
fundamentals.betaCaption: About {beta} as jumpy as the market
fundamentals.tableCaption: The facts we hold for this stock, straight from the snapshot.

stats.capNote: what the whole company is worth

stress.title: How could a bad year feel?
stress.caption: Not a replay of 2008 or any real crisis — these are illustrative rough years built from your own risk. Each pair of bars sinks below a normal year: a plain stock index falls further, and your mix falls less, because bonds and gold cushion the drop.
stress.ariaLabel: A bar chart of illustrative rough-year losses at three rarities: for each, a plain stock index's deeper fall and your mix's shallower fall, both below a 0% line for a normal year. The same data is available as a table.
stress.tableCaption: The same rough years as a table: how far a plain stock index and your mix each fall, and how much less yours falls.
stress.eqResult: In a rough year — the kind you'd see about once in 20 — your mix might fall about {loss}. A plain all-stock index would fall further, so your bonds and gold cushion the drop by roughly {cushion}.
stress.eqFormulaAlt: The loss equals the average return mu, plus z times the risk sigma.
stress.eqSymLoss: The return in that rough year — negative, so a fall.
stress.eqSymZ: How rare the year is, in risk-swings — about −1.65 for a 1-in-20 year.
stress.eqSymSigma: Your one-year swing — how much a single year can vary (about 10.4%).
stress.eqExampleAlt: 5.5% plus negative 1.65 times 10.4% is about negative 11.6%.
stress.eqExampleCaption: A plain stock index swings more (about 15%), so the same rough year hits it harder — which is the whole point of spreading out.
stress.eqWhyLabel: Why show a bad year at all
stress.eqWhy: Knowing the rough years in advance is how you hold on through them instead of selling at the bottom. A cushioned fall is easier to sit through than a plain index's.

rolling.title: Does holding longer calm the ride?
rolling.caption: The green band is the range of yearly returns you might average over each holding length — a rough run at the bottom, a good one at the top. Over one year the range is wide; the longer you hold, the more good and bad years cancel out, so the band narrows and even a rough run drifts above 0%.
rolling.ariaLabel: A shaded band of annualised returns that narrows as the holding length grows from one to ten years, with the expected-return line through it and a marker where a rough run first clears 0%. The same data is available as a table.
rolling.legendBand: Range of outcomes (80% of the time)
rolling.legendBreakeven: Where a rough run turns positive
rolling.tableCaption: The same range as a table: for a few holding lengths, a rough run, the expected return, and a good run.

stats.breakevenNote: past here, even a rough run tends to be positive

rolling.eqResult: Over one year the swing is about {sigma1} either side of the {mean} average; over ten it shrinks to about {sigma10} — so past about {years} years, even a rough run tends to land above 0%.
rolling.eqFormulaAlt: The swing over n years equals the one-year swing sigma divided by the square root of n.
rolling.eqSymSigmaN: The swing over an n-year hold — how much the average yearly return can vary.
rolling.eqExampleAlt: 10.4% divided by the square root of 10 is about 3.3%.
rolling.eqExampleCaption: The average return itself doesn't change with time — only the range around it tightens.
rolling.eqWhy: Good and bad years partly cancel out the longer you hold, so the average steadies. It's not a guarantee, but it's why long-term investing is calmer than short-term.

benchmark.title: Am I ahead of just buying the index?
benchmark.caption: Both lines are expected paths, not a track record: your mix and a plain 60/40 mix of stocks and bonds. Your mix leans a little more into stocks, so it expects a bit more growth — and takes a bit more risk. The shaded gap is how far ahead your expected path runs, and it widens as that small edge compounds.
benchmark.ariaLabel: Two projected balance lines over time — your mix and a plain 60/40 reference mix, both labelled expected paths — with the growing gap between them shaded. The same data is available as a table.
benchmark.tableCaption: The same comparison as a table: for each year, your expected balance, the 60/40's, and the gap between them.
benchmark.eqResult: Both paths use the same growth formula — your mix compounds at about {yourReturn} a year, the 60/40 at about {refReturn}. That small edge compounds to about {gap} more by {year} — for a bit more risk along the way.
benchmark.eqFormulaAlt: Future value equals P times one plus r, all raised to the power n, plus C times the quantity one plus r to the power n minus one, divided by r.
benchmark.eqSymFV: Future value — the balance a mix's expected path grows to.
benchmark.eqSymC: Contribution — what you add each month (CHF 300).
benchmark.eqSymR: Monthly growth rate — about 5% a year for your mix, a little less for the 60/40.
benchmark.eqSymN: Number of months you stay invested.
benchmark.eqExampleAlt: Using the example numbers, the future value works out to about 100,000 Swiss francs.
benchmark.eqExampleCaption: That's your mix's middle path. The 60/40 uses the same formula at a slightly lower rate, so its line sits just below.
benchmark.eqWhy: A plain 60/40 is a fair, low-effort benchmark. Beating it on the expected path is only worth it if you're comfortable with the extra risk — which the rough-year and rolling-return cards show.

Charts.tablesSectionIntro: The plan as tables you can read line by line: every holding sorted the way you want, where your money sits by type, and which holdings pay you cash along the way. Sample numbers; your real holdings arrive with your plan.

tables.viewGroup: Switch between the visual table and a plain table of the same numbers

holdings.title: What exactly do you hold?
holdings.caption: Every holding in one sortable table — tap a column heading to rank by weight, risk, or trend. The little line is an illustrative path from each holding's own expected return and swing, not a real market move, with its net change spelled out beside it.
holdings.tableCaption: Your holdings with their weight, share of risk, and illustrative trend.
holdings.ariaLabel: A sortable table of your holdings, each with its weight, its share of risk, and an illustrative-trend sparkline. Tap a column heading to sort.

exposure.title: Where does your money sit?
exposure.caption: Your money grouped by investment type — global equity, bonds, gold, and the rest — ranked biggest first. (Detailed industry sectors aren't in our data yet.)
exposure.tableCaption: Your exposure by investment type, biggest first.
exposure.ariaLabel: A ranked table of your exposure by investment type, each with its share of the plan and how many holdings make it up.

income.title: Which holdings pay you?
income.caption: Some holdings pay cash out to you (a dividend); others reinvest it for you inside the fund. This shows which is which — we don't hold yield figures, so there are no percentages to promise.
income.tableCaption: Each holding — whether it pays you income or reinvests it — and its weight.
income.ariaLabel: How your holdings split between those that pay income out and those that reinvest it, with each side's share of the plan.
income.barAria: {income} of the plan pays income out to you; {reinvest} reinvests it.

stats.payingNote: cash paid out to you along the way
